sentences.text: My name is Eric Flagg, and my date of birth is December 12, 1916.
I was born in Heidelberg, Germany.
My name at birth was Eric Flegenheimer.
Should I spell it in English?
F-L-E-G-E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R.
My mother's name was Rosa, maiden name Leiter, L-E-I-T-E-R.
Isidore Flegenheimer.
Yes, I did.
It was Ruth, R-U-T-H.
Well, I lost my father when I was only 12 years old, or I should say 12 years young.
And it is really amazing how much I do remember about my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a wonderful man.
He had a tremendous sense of humor.
He had a bad temper.
He had tremendous amount of common sense.
He was warm.
And the interesting thing is when my son was about 8 or 12 years old, about the age I was when my dad died, many, many things which he used to say came back to my mind and my memory.
I don't know whether I should give you a few examples.
I do?
Well, my father was a manufacturer of cigars, and he did a lot of traveling in small communities, and my father was quite a religious man.
And some day, someone asked him, Herr Flegenheimer, how can you do it in small communities and not live up to the dietary laws of our faith?
And my father said to him, a sin can never come into your mouth, only out.
That, I remember.
You want to know some more things?
Really?
OK.
My father used to say, dear God, don't give me what I'm capable to endure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He also used to say, I don't bet.
I swear.
It is cheaper.
And some day, someone asked him, how do you feel about Hitler?
Hitler was not in power at the time, but he was big political force in the time.
And how do you feel about Hitler?
And he said, I wish I could eat as much as I like to slob about him.
So it's the little things.
I could go on, and on, and on.
They're important to me.
You have wonderful memories of your father.
I remember when we spoke a few weeks ago.
Oh, many, many more.
But, anyway, my mother died in 1941 in Lisbon, Portugal.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was a wonderful lady.
She also had a temper and was very much interested in art, literature art, and was very loving, and was a wonderful lady.
Yes.

I made that remark to you because I couldn't say it somewhere else because the idea-- American Jewish princess does not exist somewhere else, but she definitely was a German Jewish princess, absolutely.
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unfortunately, I have to talk about the funerals because my father was the youngest of 13 children, and my mother was the youngest of nine, so everybody was much older.
I have attended many funerals, some weddings of cousins. 50Th birthday of my father, I remember, was 1925.
He was born in 1875.
Yeah, there were quite some family gatherings.
The two families didn't get along too well.
My parents did, but the families didn't get along too well.
My father comes from a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very small community where he was born, had an entirely different background.
My mother's parents were-- came from a larger city.
He was a banker.
And both families had a feeling that their son and their daughter were marrying someone below their own level.
[LAUGHTER] It sounds ridiculous, but that's the way it was.
But they had a happy marriage.
That's important.
Well, obviously, I was enrolled in grammar school when I was 10 years old.
I went to grammar school from-- excuse me, it was six years old.
I went to grammar school from six to 10.
After 10, I came in what is called here high school.
I do remember, and I don't think you know what the name means.
I'm sorry for that remark.
Pestalozzi School.
Pestalozzi was a Swiss educator in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18th or 17th century, and he was very much involved in the educational system in his country.
And in the German-speaking part of Europe, like Austria, Switzerland, and Germany, many schools are named after him.
I beg your pardon?
Well, my main interest was really playing soccer.
I was more interested in soccer than in my schooling.
And there were no specific experiences between six and 10.
I had many good friends.
I learned reading, writing, arithmetic and had a good time.
I would say minimum of 90%.
That's correct.
I mean, Heidelberg at that time was a community of about 70,000 people.
There were but 1,400 Jews, so 2%.
I would say 90% of my friends, school buddies, or social acquaintances and friends of my parents and my sister were non-Jewish.
Never.
Never.
Absolutely never.
What you call your high school.
Well, you really want me to go into that?
OK.
There is a gymnasium and a realgymnasium.
In the gymnasium, you learn Greek.
In the realgymnasium, you learn Latin and French.
And in the gymnasium, you learnt Greek and Latin, but no French.
And in all other areas, there was no difference.
So the school I went to was realgymnasium, but I forgot the name.
Well, I was 16 years old when Hitler became chancellor of Germany.
It was January 30, 1933.
I was 16 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Some.

Very, very few of my fellow boys that goes for school and for the sport club to which I belonged-- some took a cautious negative attitude towards me because they were probably scared and worried to associate closely with a Jew.
There was the minority.
The overwhelming majority didn't mean anything one way or the other, and the other minority were boys who were much nicer to me than before because subconsciously, they had a bad conscience.
In other words, a minority took a negative attitude, another minority a very, very positive attitude, and the overwhelming majority, what's for dinner tomorrow?
Couldn't care less.
Do you think your parents felt the same changes among their friends?
Did they feel isolated, do you think?
I'm sorry.
Your father was gone by then, so your mother was alone.
Yes.
Not really.
Not really.
No.
There was only a few.
I had especially one boy by the name of Alex Rammermeier.
He came to my house to play chess with me in SS uniform.
And my Aunt Frida almost had a heart attack when that young man in SS uniform-- I was at that time 17 or 18, or he couldn't be in the SS.
He was two years older.
The son, the youngest son of my Aunt Frida, who lived in consulate, he committed suicide on April 1, 1933, which was the so-called Boycott Saturday.
And he committed suicide.

And he left a note behind him, and he said, I cannot think of any way to appeal to the conscience and the intelligence and the goodwill of my non-Jewish friends but shaking them up by taking my own life.
And his mother was visiting with us, and when that guy came in in the SS uniform, she was very shaky.
No.
I had no personal, negative experiences as a Jew in Heidelberg at all.
After I was thrown out of the-- of my school-- not individually, but Jewish boys couldn't be in that school anymore.
It was immediately after the Olympic Games.
So I would say--
'36 It was after the Olympic Games. '36.
Then I became a member of a Jewish sport club, and we had a little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a soccer club.
And we had a soccer field where we played.
And one day, the Stormtroopers, during the game, walked through our soccer field and were singing a certain song, which I don't want to translate into English.
And there were two guys in that group who were walking through, and they both came to me in the next day and apologized.
I have personally, personally, never been exposed to any [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] hateful attitude or even insulting attitude.
My personal-- you asked me my personal reaction?
I'm sorry?
Did you ask me what my personal reaction was?
I was angry.
You asked me a while ago, was I scared or uncomfortable?
I was terribly angry.

Here is that stupid guy who is not even a German-- come from Austria-- and he has the nerve, speaking in poor German with a terrible accent, and tell people that the Jews are the misfortune of Germany, and we are not Germans, and we don't belong here.
And my family lived in Germany maybe for 1,500 years, probably.
I don't know.
And there were Jewish communities in Germany in the entire area where the Romans came.
That means southwest Germany and western Germany, along the Rhine River, the Danube, and so on.
And here, that guy is telling all those terrible things.
I never felt scared.
I never felt threatened.
I thought it won't take very long.
The German people got to get wise to that person.
And, of course, that changed in 1938 after Kristallnacht.
But till that time, I was angry, and rightly so.
No, I wouldn't say everybody else, no.
After all, there was a very strong [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish emigration out of Germany.

There were a little bit over 600,000 Jews lived in Germany when Hitler became chancellor, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beginning of the war, '39, in August or July, there were between 50,000 to 300,000.
So half of the German Jews left Germany as emigrants between-- the emigration was very strong in '33.
Strong in '34, '35, '36.
A little '37 increasing. '38, very strong.
And '39-- couldn't get out anymore after July anyway.
So my personal attitude was not necessarily typical for all German Jews.

I read while I was doing some research that in '33 a lot of the Jews from Germany to the Netherlands, but in '34-- '34 was relatively quiet as far as anti-Semitic actions, and a lot of Jews returned to Germany.
I doubt very much whether there were a lot.
Yes, some, I'm sure.
A lot, I don't think so.
I don't think so.
My sister left for Portugal in 1937.
Yes.
No.
Her husband was a German Jew.
They got married in Heidelberg, Germany.
And a few weeks after their marriage, he emigrated to Portugal, which was pre-arranged, and he started a business there.
And she followed him, oh, maybe four, five, six months later, approximately.
Yes.
My mother was very, very lucky.
She got a permit to enter Portugal in June 1939.
That's when she left Germany, went to Portugal, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made the trip via Holland.
That's where I met her.
She went from Heidelberg, Amsterdam, Lisbon.
That's where we met, so for the last time there.
And in June '41, she died in Lisbon.
In other words, three years later.
What I was doing?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I was kicked out of school in '37, something like that.
It was not an individual kick-out.
I went to a private school.
I learned a lot of English.
I was very much interested in playing soccer, did a lot of gambling.
I did a few other things which should not be on tape, I guess.
And I lived the life of an irresponsible, spoiled, rich Jewish kid giving a damn about anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my life from 1937 till after '38, when I fled to Holland.
I couldn't care about anything.
I just had a fabulous time.
No.
Well, the lifestyle has not changed, but everything what we owned-- my father was dead.
I mean, our factory was taken away.
I was not aware of it.
I mean, you're a young kid, you know?
Your only interest is the income.
The income did not change, but there was no substance.
We were living from whatever was left.
And I left Germany for Holland with 10 marks in my pocket, and my mother the same.
So there was nothing.
We were very poor, but we didn't realize it.
Now, did you leave because your mother left, or did you leave because you knew there were terrible problems ahead?
Did you feel there were terrible problems ahead for you?
Well, the Kristallnacht on November the 10th woke me up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.
I mean, everybody's aware of the fact what happened.
Most synagogues were burned.
Most Jewish stores or offices were destroyed.
And an overwhelming majority of Jewish males between 18 and 45 were arrested and came to concentration camps.
That was not-- they were not killed there.
A few-- and they were discharged anywhere between two to six weeks later.
They came to me also to arrest me, but somehow, they didn't because there was a young girl by the name of Marian Strauss who put-- she was five years old.
We lived in the guest house at the time.
And they arrested her dad.
And then they wanted to take me, and she put her arms around my legs and started to cry and told the Stormtroopers, not Eric, not Eric.
Don't take Eric.
They didn't take me.
And then I went to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] am I talking too much?
No.
No.
And there was a terrible situation.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that particular time, there was a business associate of my father from Holland was in Heidelberg.
He was selling tobacco.
My father bought tobacco from him.
And he saw what's happening, and he was terribly upset.
And he said, Eric, I want you to come.
I'm leaving Heidelberg.
I cannot stay.
I don't want to do any business.
I'm going tonight by train to Holland, and I will see to it that on the border, there will be a permit for you entering, and I want you to leave.
Come to Holland the day after tomorrow.
No.
And every Jew in Heidelberg of my age was arrested.
I went the next day to the Gestapo.
It is so dumb, what I did, that I don't believe it, but it's true.
I went to the Gestapo, and I showed them my passport, which I had.
And I said, I want you to put a stamp in my passport with today's date.
And they said, why?
I said, well, when I come to the border and they'll think I escaped arrest, but if you put a stamp on it, they'll know I came here.
They said, we don't do that.
Get out of here.
I say, well, if you want the Jews to leave Germany, if you want me to go, you put it in, OK?
They did.
I went the next day, the Rheingold Express, fabulous luxury train, to Holland.
The German border city, the name is [INAUDIBLE].
They came and stomped over who was custom guy came in, a young man, maybe a year older than I.
He looked at my passport [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and made the motion with the thumb.
Go.
I come to Holland.
It is about two minutes later.
I go to the border police.
They had no permit for me, so the Dutch policeman said, well, you have no permit.
You have to go back.
You have to go back.

Now, I'd like you to know, by the way, that it was policy of Germany at that time that any Jew who left Germany and came back at that time automatically [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was thrown into a concentration camp.
So the Dutch policeman did not let me in, but he got off his chair, left his office, and left the door open.
I didn't realize what he was doing, but he said to me, get out of here.
I didn't.
I did not.
It didn't even occur to me.
I was very stupid.
So half an hour, the next train, I go back to Germany, and what happens?
The same Stormtrooper looked at me.
You're back?
I told him yes, my papers were not in order right now.
They were not complete.
They couldn't let me in.
What are you going to do?
I say, well, I go in the border city in a little hotel, and I called Holland right away and let them know where Amsterdam can send me the papers.
And the Stormtrooper said to me, I wouldn't do that because there are many [INAUDIBLE] at the border cities.
Why don't you go back to Heidelberg where you belong?
Inform the people in Holland where you are?
And I said to him, I can't because I have only 10 marks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He went in his pocket and gave me 20 or 30 marks, I forgot, enough for me to buy a ticket to go back to Heidelberg, and gave me his name.
His name was Gramlich.
I forgot his first name.
And he sent me back to Heidelberg.
I am convinced that that's not the only time he did that.
He was a decent, wonderful human being who saved my life.

And when I was back in Heidelberg, I sent the money, and-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the first days of '39-- with other words, about four weeks later-- I got my permit to Holland and left in January, five, six, seven, eight, nine-- forgot-- for Holland.
Yes.
What happened?
I came into a camp by the name [INAUDIBLE].
And that was a camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] basically for young scientists from Germany and from Austria who were learning a trade there-- agriculture, gardening, cabinet making, and stuff like that.
And I did not belong there because I was not a scientist, and I was not preparing to go in an Aliyah-- [INAUDIBLE] to Israel, but they let me in.
And that's where I was.
Till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May '41.
With other words, about two years and two months.
It was actually my college education.
I did not want to do any gardening or any agriculture or any cabinet making.
I became a night watchman.
